## 1. The lower bound

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

### 1.1. A refinement and a cevian-lengthening fact

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset \partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so it lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Hence every cevian from \(a\) to a point of the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

We may refine \(\mathcal T\) so that it is face-to-face. Indeed, two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in their interiors: near a transverse crossing, the interiors of the incident triangles would occupy two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines, and any two such half-planes overlap in a sector, contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors. Two nonboundary edges also cannot partially overlap: a nonhorizontal line meets each of \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in a unique point, so two collinear nonboundary edges have the same endpoints and coincide.

Thus the only non-face-to-face phenomenon is subdivision along boundary edges. Subdivide every boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on it, and cone the new boundary subvertices to the opposite vertex. Local finiteness guarantees only finitely many such vertices on each compact boundary edge. The refinement is still clean, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. Boundary subedges are strictly shortened, and the new nonboundary edges are cevians, hence are strictly lengthened by the preceding paragraph. Replacing \(\mathcal T\) by this refinement, we assume from now on that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

---

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band and its PL embedding

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=([0,\beta]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary is a circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Since \(f(G_\beta p)=f(p)\), \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3 .
\]
The orbit condition says precisely that \(F\) is injective. Because \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\): even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers translate horizontally by an odd multiple of \(\beta\) and reverse \(y\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then, since \(f|_\Delta\) is affine on a genuine two-dimensional triangle, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(w=G_\beta^{-k}z\in\Delta\), and \(f(w)=f(z)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(w=z\), impossible because the \(G_\beta\)-action is free.

Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting the compact rectangle \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). By local finiteness, only finitely many triangles meet that rectangle. Hence the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite, and \(F\) is a finite PL embedding.

---

### 1.3. The leaf graph

Inside every clean triangle, consider the family of cevians joining the vertex opposite the boundary edge to points of the boundary edge. These cevians will be called leaves.

For the finite quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), define a graph \(\Lambda_M\) as follows. Each quotient triangle contributes one closed interval parametrizing its leaves. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides of the triangle. Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding interval endpoints.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangle sides, counted with multiplicity, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a single fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes, so the whole fan belongs to one \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected surface \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1 .
\]

Let \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) be the analogous leaf graph upstairs in \(\Sigma\). The same fan argument, using local finiteness, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The deck action of \(\langle G_\beta\rangle\) acts on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and the quotient graph is \(\Lambda_M\).

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
An even nonzero power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it cannot preserve the leaf. An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the endpoints, forcing
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta
\quad\text{and}\quad
-s=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Thus the deck action on leaves is free. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering map. Since the orbit of any lifted leaf under \(\langle G_\beta\rangle\) is infinite, this covering has infinite degree. Therefore the connected covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to S^1\) is the universal cover:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R .
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Each leaf is strictly lengthened, hence its image is a nondegenerate straight segment and determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Choose one lifted circuit, namely a path in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from a leaf \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\) projecting homeomorphically onto \(\Lambda_M\). If \(\ell\) has endpoints \(b\in y=0\) and \(t\in y=1\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation is reversed. Thus one full circuit of \(\Lambda_M\) changes an oriented support line into the same support line with the opposite orientation.

We now use the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous representatives \((u(t),m(t))\) for \(0\le t\le1\), and extend them to all \(t\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
These are well-defined on \(S^1\times[0,1]\). We have
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
is a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). The bottom circle \(\delta=0\) is constant, so it has degree \(0\). On the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
If \(\gamma:S^1\to S^1\) satisfies \(\gamma(s+\frac12)=-\gamma(s)\), then \(\gamma\) has odd degree: choosing a lift \(\theta:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) of \(\gamma\), we get
\[
\theta\left(s+\frac12\right)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi k
\]
for a constant integer \(k\), hence
\[
\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2\pi(2k+1).
\]
Thus the middle circle has odd degree. But it is homotopic in the cylinder to the bottom circle, which has degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the corresponding directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma to the support-line path around \(\Lambda_M\), we obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

### 1.4. The metric estimate

Let the support lines of \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) be perpendicular and meet at \(P\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge \operatorname{length}(F(B)).
\]
Every lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and every leaf is strictly lengthened. Therefore
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) and \(H\) be the two boundary arcs of \(\partial M_\beta\) between the endpoints of \(T\), with \(q\in D\). Since \(\partial M_\beta\) has flat length \(2\beta\), write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the boundary circle by arclength so that a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), while a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u+\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). Then the two arc lengths between the endpoints of \(T\) imply
\[
s\equiv t \pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), it follows that
\[
|s|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus the flat length of \(T\) is at least \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

The boundary arc \(H\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened: indeed, subdivide the arc at all boundary vertices of the finite quotient triangulation; on each positive-length subarc, \(F\) is affine with shortening factor \(<1\). Therefore
\[
L\le \ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes Euclidean length of the image path. Combining this with (1),
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

The boundary arc \(D\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}<\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), impossible.

Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

---

## 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

We use the following standard form of Sadowsky’s construction.

**Sadowsky construction.** Let \(l,b,d>0\) satisfy
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)
\]
has an embedded path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realized surface consists of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces supported on cylinders of diameters \(d,d,2d\). Each piece is the isometric development of a Euclidean planar piece onto either a plane or a circular cylinder; adjacent pieces meet along full-width generator segments, and the planes are tangent to the adjacent cylinders. Consequently the ruling foliation extends continuously across seams. The construction has a continuous full-width foliation by straight rulings, and strict inequality gives positive separation between nonincident compact pieces.

Choose
\[
\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta).
\]
Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose \(d>0\) so small that
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Applying Sadowsky’s construction with \(l=\mu\) and \(b=\frac12\), and shifting the vertical coordinate to \([0,1]\), gives a lift
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with deck map
\[
H_\mu(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y)
\]
such that
\[
E_\mu\circ H_\mu=E_\mu
\]
and
\[
E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_\mu^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L=\mu c\) lift
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

---

### 2.1. Ruling coordinates and bounded horizontal span

For the scaled Sadowsky lift \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\), every bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique full-width ruling whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Write
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
The function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight segments \(R_{x_1}\) and \(R_{x_2}\) would intersect or share a top endpoint, contradicting the fact that the rulings form a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. Since every top boundary point lies on a unique ruling, \(h\) is onto; therefore \(h\) is an increasing homeomorphism.

The deck transformation sends rulings to rulings. Since
\[
H(x,0)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(h(x),c)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
we have
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
The top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is \((x+L,c)\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Because \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)>x\) for all \(x\).

The horizontal spans of the rulings are uniformly bounded. Fix \(x_0\), and set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Since
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
the same bound holds on every \(\phi\)-translate of \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\). Those translates cover \(\mathbb R\), so
\[
|h(x)-x|\le S
\]
for all \(x\in\mathbb R\).

---

### 2.2. A PL secant approximation

We need a controlled PL approximation of the Sadowsky lift.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a scaled Sadowsky lift with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Let \(S\) be the global bound for the horizontal spans of the rulings. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)
   \]
   for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);

2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;

3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]

4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
which contains all seam rulings in one fundamental interval and is fine enough that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for all \(i\). Extend it by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The consecutive rulings \(B_iT_i\) and \(B_{i+1}T_{i+1}\) bound a clean quadrilateral
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]
For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) using the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Since
\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N},
\]
the image under \(H\) uses the opposite diagonal in \(Q_{i+N}\). Extend this triangulation by \(H\)-invariance. The result is an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Then \(F_0\circ H=F_0\).

We choose the partition sufficiently fine so that the quotient PL map is embedded. Work on the compact Sadowsky quotient band. Consider a sequence of such partitions with the maximum of the quantities
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
\]
tending to \(0\), always including the seam rulings. The corresponding secant maps converge uniformly to the Sadowsky embedding. Indeed, let \(p\in Q_i\), and let \(R_x\) be the ruling through \(p\), with bottom endpoint \(B_x=(x,0)\) and top endpoint \(T_x=(h(x),c)\). If \(p\) has height \(Y=vc\), then
\[
p=(1-v)B_x+vT_x.
\]
The Sadowsky map is linear on every ruling, so
\[
E(p)=(1-v)E(B_x)+vE(T_x).
\]
On the triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\) containing \(p\), the barycentric weights of the top vertices sum to \(v\), and the barycentric weights of the bottom vertices sum to \(1-v\). All bottom vertices of that triangle are within flat distance \(<\rho\) of \(B_x\), and all top vertices are within flat distance \(<\rho\) of \(T_x\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric, Euclidean distances between their images are at most those flat distances. Hence \(|F_0(p)-E(p)|\le \rho\). Thus the secant maps converge uniformly as \(\rho\to0\).

For sufficiently fine partitions, local injectivity holds. On planar pieces, the secant map agrees with the original affine isometry. On a cylindrical piece, consecutive generator rulings with angular separation \(<\pi\) determine planar chord strips forming an embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder; adjacent strips meet only along their shared generator. Near a seam, choose coordinates with the seam as an axis and the common tangent plane as \(\{z=0\}\). The planar piece lies in \(\{z=0\}\), while the cylindrical piece and its sufficiently fine chordal approximants lie on one side of that plane, meeting it only along the seam. Therefore no local crossing is introduced near seams.

These local statements give a finite open cover \(U_1,\dots,U_m\) of the compact quotient band such that the quotient secant map is injective on each \(U_j\). Let
\[
K=\{(p,q): p,q \text{ do not both lie in any }U_j\}.
\]
Then \(K\) is compact and avoids the diagonal. Since the original Sadowsky realization is embedded, there is \(\eta>0\) such that
\[
|E(p)-E(q)|\ge \eta
\]
for all \((p,q)\in K\). By uniform convergence, after refining further we have \(|F_0-E|<\eta/3\), so no pair in \(K\) is identified by \(F_0\). Hence the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back gives quotient-injectivity.

Now verify the edge estimates. A boundary edge maps to the chord between two points on a boundary arc of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface, so its image length is at most its flat length.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|,
\]
because rulings are mapped isometrically to straight segments.

For a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also
\[
|E(T_{i+1})-E(T_i)|\le |T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&> r-\rho \\
&> d-2\rho \\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type is identical, using \(|B_{i+1}-B_i|<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D.
\]
The other diagonal type satisfies the same estimate. \(\square\)

---

### 2.3. Realizing every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta),
\]
and define
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Start with the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac1\lambda.
\]
Set
\[
c=a\lambda>1,\qquad L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Scale the width-one Sadowsky band by \(c\). Its width is \(c\), its length is \(L\), and its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\). Apply the PL secant lemma with
\[
D=cD_\mu .
\]

We need a strict length margin. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
When \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and
\[
\Delta_1(s)=\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0
\]
because \(\lambda>1\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m,
\]
and apply the PL secant lemma with this \(\varepsilon\).

Define the anisotropic affine map
\[
\mathcal A:\Sigma\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
\mathcal A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
\mathcal A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H(\mathcal A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Let
\[
\mathcal T=\mathcal A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ\mathcal A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\) of length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(\mathcal A p)-F_0(\mathcal A q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). By the secant lemma,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), so \(0\le s<D_\mu\). Since \(\mathcal A(p)\) and \(\mathcal A(q)\) lie on opposite boundary components,
\[
|\mathcal A(p)-\mathcal A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\). Since \(c/a=\lambda\),
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
The secant lemma gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Because \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(\mathcal A p)=F_0(\mathcal A q) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \mathcal A p=H^k(\mathcal A q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

Combining the lower and upper bounds, \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the set of realized numbers is
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

As written, the main mathematical strategy is sound, but the leaf-graph and PL-embedding steps need the additional details listed above.

**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the infimum of the realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its **boundary edge**, and call the opposite vertex its **apex**.

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A=f|_\tau\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since the boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0.
\]
Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened by \(f\).

We may refine the triangulation so that it is face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: near a transverse crossing, the two incident triangles would occupy overlapping open sectors, contradicting disjointness of interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then both join \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so the supporting line determines both endpoints uniquely; hence the edges coincide. Thus only boundary edges can fail to meet face-to-face.

For each boundary edge, subdivide it at all boundary vertices lying on it, and cone the resulting subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness guarantees that each compact boundary edge is subdivided into finitely many subedges. The construction is \(G_\beta\)-equivariant. Boundary subedges are still strictly shortened, and the cevian-lengthening argument shows all new nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened. Hence, after replacing the triangulation by this refinement, we may assume the triangulation is face-to-face and \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every triangle.

### 1.2. The quotient triangulation

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is a compact Möbius band. The orbit condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The same orbit condition says \(F\) is injective. Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus a nontrivial even power moves points horizontally by at least \(2\beta\), while a nontrivial odd power moves points horizontally by at least \(\beta\).

It follows that \(f|_\tau\) is injective on every triangle \(\tau\). If not, then the affine linear part of \(f|_\tau\) would have rank \(<2\), so there would be distinct arbitrarily close points \(p,q\in\tau\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would imply \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), impossible when \(|p-q|<\beta\).

Also, no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\tau\cap G_\beta^k\tau\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\tau\). Since \(f(z)=f(w)\) and \(f|_\tau\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the \(G_\beta\)-action.

A compact fundamental rectangle, for example \([0,2\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness. Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting this rectangle, so the quotient triangulation of \(M\) is finite and has no self-identifications of cells.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles. Indeed, if \(e\) is a nonboundary edge and \(z\) is an interior point of \(e\), then a sufficiently small disk in \(\operatorname{int}\Sigma\) centered at \(z\) is cut by \(e\) into two open half-disks. One incident triangle occupies one side. Since the union of the triangles is all of \(\Sigma\), the other side must also be occupied by a triangle; disjointness of interiors implies that at most one triangle occupies each side. Hence exactly two triangles meet along \(e\).

Finally, every nontrivial boundary subarc of \(M\) is strictly shortened by \(F\). Indeed, on each quotient boundary edge \(e\), the map \(F\) is affine with some length factor \(\rho_e<1\). A nontrivial boundary subarc decomposes into finitely many edge-subsegments \(I_m\), so
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)=\sum_m \rho_{e_m}\ell_0(I_m)
<
\sum_m \ell_0(I_m)
=
\ell_0(\gamma).
\]

### 1.3. The leaf graph

In each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. Glue endpoints of such intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The resulting finite graph is denoted \(\Lambda_M\). Its points parametrize leaves, namely cevians in the quotient triangulation.

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) corresponds to a nonboundary edge, and every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles; hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles correspond to intervals meeting at the same vertex of \(\Lambda_M\), so they both lie in \(U_C\) or both lie outside it. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so again either the whole fan lies in \(U_C\) or none of it does. Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed because it is a finite subcomplex. Since \(M\) is connected, \(U_C=M\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. A finite connected graph with every vertex of valence \(2\) is a circle:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Define the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) analogously upstairs. The same open-and-closed argument applies; closedness now follows from local finiteness. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M\) induces a graph covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it fixes the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore the covering has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) and \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, we have
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]
Choose a parameter \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) so that \(G_\beta\) acts by \(s\mapsto s+1\).

Orient each lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian-lengthening lemma, its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, hence determines an oriented support line \(L(s)\subset\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously with \(s\). Since \(G_\beta\) reverses bottom and top while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\),
\[
L(s+1)=L(s)^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

### 1.4. An oriented-line lemma

We use the following elementary fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L(s)\), \(s\in\mathbb R\), be a continuous family of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) satisfying \(L(s+1)=L(s)^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there exist \(s\in\mathbb R\) and \(0<\delta<1\) such that \(L(s)\) and \(L(s+\delta)\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) with
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and for the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12).
\]
Thus the circle map \(s\mapsto \phi(s,\tfrac12)\) has odd degree. Indeed, if \(\phi(s,\tfrac12)=e^{i\theta(s)}\), then
\[
\theta(s+\tfrac12)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for some fixed integer \(m\), so
\[
\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2(2m+1)\pi.
\]
Hence the degree is \(2m+1\), odd. But this map is homotopic through the maps \(\phi(\cdot,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le\frac12\), to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction.

Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the directions are perpendicular. If the two lines have representatives \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), this vanishing says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. The lemma follows.

Applying the lemma, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

### 1.5. Winding control

Let \(J\) be a lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]

If \(t=\beta\), then the endpoints are related by \(G_\beta\), so their \(f\)-images coincide, contradicting the fact that \(f(J)\) is a nondegenerate segment. Similarly, \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), translate horizontally so that \(J\) joins \((0,0)\) to \((t,1)\). Then \(G_\beta J\), oriented from bottom to top, joins \((t+\beta,0)\) to \((\beta,1)\). The two segments intersect in their relative interiors at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Use the boundary coordinate \(u\in\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) on \(\partial M\) in which \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\), while \((v,1)\) has coordinate \(v-\beta\). Thus the endpoints of \(J\) have boundary coordinates
\[
x,\qquad x+t-\beta.
\]
The two boundary arcs between them have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so it interchanges these two lengths.

### 1.6. The metric contradiction

The support lines of \(T\) and \(B\) are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Since \(F:M\to\mathbb R^3\) is an embedding and the relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M\), the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename them if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior.

Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Also,
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
Since \(B\) is a bottom-to-top segment, its flat length is at least \(1\), and by cevian lengthening,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Therefore
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))>1.
\]

Let \(a,c\in\partial M\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and put
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
After replacing this lift by its \(G_\beta\)-translate if necessary, arrange that the boundary arc \(D\) from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc; then
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the image path \(F(D)\) goes from \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\), so
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, in the plane spanned by \(Q\) and the line \(AC\), place
\[
A=(-L/2,0),\qquad C=(L/2,0),\qquad Q=(s,y).
\]
Then the sum
\[
\sqrt{(s+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(s-L/2)^2+y^2}
\]
is minimized at \(s=0\), giving \(\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Therefore \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

This proves necessity.

## 2. Sufficiency: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Now fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
b=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\qquad
L_*=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L_*<b\). Choose
\[
L_*<\sigma<b,
\]
and define
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_*,\,b-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\,\beta/12\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation

Define a periodic sequence \((\ell_j)\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
b-2\lambda, & j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda, & j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{8}\ell_j=3b=2\beta.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Thus
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]
Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),
\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
The \(X_j\) are strictly ordered along the bottom boundary, and the \(Y_j\) are strictly ordered along the top boundary.

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(y\), the left side \(X_jY_j\) has \(x\)-coordinate
\[
(1-y)c_j+y(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and these coordinates form a strictly increasing bi-infinite sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\). Hence the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]
This is a locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Moreover,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient Möbius band, let \(v_j\) be the boundary vertex represented by \(X_j\), with indices read modulo \(9\). The quotient has faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) of side length \(\sigma\) in the plane \(z=0\), centered at the origin:
\[
p_0=\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3},0,0\right),
\]
\[
p_1=\left(-\frac{\sigma}{2\sqrt3},\frac{\sigma}{2},0\right),
\]
\[
p_2=\left(-\frac{\sigma}{2\sqrt3},-\frac{\sigma}{2},0\right).
\]
Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For \(r>0\) and \(z\in\mathbb R\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=r p_i+z e_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small, and set
\[
q_i^A=q_i(1,0),
\qquad
q_i^M=q_i(1-\eta,h),
\qquad
q_i^C=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Because \(q_i^A,q_i^M,q_i^C\to p_i\) as \(\eta,h\to0\), we may arrange that:

1. If \(i\neq k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   \sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}<|q_i^U-q_k^V|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
   \]
2. If \(i=k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   |q_i^U-q_i^V|<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
   \]

Define nine target boundary vertices periodically by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0^C&q_0^M&q_0^A&
q_1^A&q_1^C&q_1^M&
q_2^M&q_2^A&q_2^C .
\end{array}
\]
Let \(F\) be the quotient PL map defined by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j
\]
and affine extension over each face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

### 2.3. The quotient PL map is embedded

Let
\[
K(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{rp_0,rp_1,rp_2\},
\]
and let
\[
S_{ij}(r)=[rp_i,rp_j].
\]
We use the following simple coordinate fact: if \(0<r_1<r_2\), then
\[
S_{12}(r_2)\cap K(r_1)=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, after rescaling, take
\[
p_0=(1,0),\qquad p_1=(-1/2,\sqrt3/2),\qquad p_2=(-1/2,-\sqrt3/2).
\]
Then \(K(r_1)\) lies in the half-plane \(x\ge -r_1/2\), while \(S_{12}(r_2)\) lies on the line \(x=-r_2/2<-r_1/2\).

Let \(\Theta_j=F(T_j)\). The three horizontal target triangles are
\[
\Theta_2=K(1),
\qquad
\Theta_5=K(1-\eta)+h e_3,
\qquad
\Theta_8=K(1+\eta)+2h e_3.
\]
The remaining six target triangles form three side panels:
\[
\Theta_1\cup\Theta_6,\qquad
\Theta_3\cup\Theta_7,\qquad
\Theta_0\cup\Theta_4.
\]
A direct barycentric-coordinate check gives the following horizontal slices. For \(0\le z\le h\),
\[
(\Theta_1\cup\Theta_6)\cap\{Z=z\}
=
S_{20}\left(1-\eta z/h\right)+z e_3.
\]
For \(0\le z\le 2h\),
\[
(\Theta_3\cup\Theta_7)\cap\{Z=z\}
=
S_{12}\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right)+z e_3.
\]
For \(h\le z\le 2h\),
\[
(\Theta_0\cup\Theta_4)\cap\{Z=z\}
=
S_{01}\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right)+z e_3.
\]

For \(0<z<h\), the slice of the whole surface is
\[
S_{20}\left(1-\eta z/h\right)
\cup
S_{12}\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right),
\]
and the first scale is smaller than the second, so the two segments are disjoint by the coordinate fact.

For \(h<z<2h\), the slice is
\[
S_{01}\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right)
\cup
S_{12}\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right).
\]
Here
\[
1+\eta z/(2h)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so these two segments are also disjoint.

At \(z=h\), the slice is
\[
K(1-\eta)\cup S_{12}(1+\eta/2),
\]
and \(S_{12}(1+\eta/2)\) is disjoint from \(K(1-\eta)\). At \(z=0\) and \(z=2h\), the side panels meet the horizontal triangles exactly along their prescribed common edges.

Since different height levels are disjoint, the target triangles have no unprescribed intersections. Therefore \(F:M\to\mathbb R^3\) is a PL embedding.

### 2.4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=b-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le b-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}\le b-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<b-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Hence every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### 2.5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Let
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
A lift of \(e_j\) is the segment from \(X_j\) to \(Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is
\[
s_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta)-c_j=d_j-\beta,
\]
where
\[
d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
From the periodic pattern,
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
b+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2b-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(b=2\beta/3\), this gives
\[
|s_j|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore the flat length of every \(e_j\) is
\[
\sqrt{1+s_j^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_*.
\]

On the target side, \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le \sigma-L_*\),
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}\ge L_*+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_*.
\]
Thus every edge \(e_j\) is strictly lengthened.

In the face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
the two nonboundary sides are \(v_jv_{j+5}=e_j\) and
\[
v_{j+1}v_{j+5}=e_{j+5}
\]
with indices modulo \(9\). Hence both nonboundary sides of every face are strictly lengthened, while the boundary side is strictly shortened. Thus \(F\) restricts to a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

### 2.6. Lift to \(\Sigma\)

Define \(f\) on the vertices of the triangulation of \(\Sigma\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The preceding edge-length estimates show that \(f\) is a squeeze map on every triangle.

Moreover,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, it follows that
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\).

Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F:M\to\mathbb R^3\). Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if their quotient classes in \(M\) are equal, which is equivalent to
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

Combining necessity and sufficiency, the realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Therefore their infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

**Claim.** With the definitions in the problem, a number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if

## 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume that \(\beta\) is realized by a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\). The orbit condition immediately gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

### 1.1. Refinement and cevian lengthening

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
Q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(Q(0)>0\) and \(Q(1)>0\). Since the boundary edge is shortened,
\[
Q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0.
\]
Thus \(Q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
Q(s)\ge (1-s)Q(0)+sQ(1)>0.
\]
Hence every cevian from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened. Also, because \(A\) is affine on \([r_0,r_1]\) and shortens that edge, every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each original triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original vertex lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subintervals to the opposite apex. A compact boundary edge meets only finitely many original triangles by local finiteness, hence contains only finitely many original vertices. Thus each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles. The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. By the cevian argument above, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

Call the refined triangulation \(\mathcal T\). It is face-to-face upstairs. Indeed, boundary-edge overlaps have been subdivided at all boundary vertices. Two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in their relative interiors, because then the interiors of the two triangles adjacent to those edges would overlap locally. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges with a common relative interior point must coincide, since a line meets each boundary component \(\mathbb R\times\{0\}\) and \(\mathbb R\times\{1\}\) in at most one point.

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
It is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the orbit condition \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For later use, note that every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus, for \(k\neq 0\),
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge \beta.
\]

We claim that \(f\) is injective on every closed refined triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\). If not, the affine linear part of \(f|_\Delta\) would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\).

It follows that no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If
\[
z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\neq0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting the fact that \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed points.

Therefore the quotient projection \(\pi:\Sigma\to M_\beta\) is injective on every closed refined triangle. Since a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Hence the quotient has a finite PL \(\Delta\)-complex structure inherited from \(\mathcal T\). Incidences are inherited from the upstairs face-to-face triangulation. In particular, every nonboundary edge has exactly two incident triangle sides, and every boundary edge has exactly one incident triangle side.

Because every boundary edge is strictly shortened by \(F\), every nontrivial boundary arc in \(\partial M_\beta\) is strictly shortened: subdivide the arc at the finitely many boundary vertices and sum the strict contractions on the resulting subarcs.

### 1.3. The leaf graph

For each triangle \(\tau\in\mathcal T\), consider the closed interval \(I_\tau\) parametrizing the cevians from its apex to points of its boundary edge. The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints. The resulting graph is the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) has valence \(2\), because every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles.

We show that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and let \(U_C\subset\Sigma\) be the union of all triangles \(\tau\) with \(I_\tau\subset C\). If a point lies in the interior of a selected triangle, it has a neighborhood in \(U_C\). If it lies in the relative interior of a selected nonboundary edge, then both adjacent triangles are selected, because their cevian intervals meet at the corresponding vertex of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If it lies in the relative interior of a selected boundary edge, it has a one-sided neighborhood in the selected triangle. Finally, at a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges; hence if one incident triangle is selected, then all incident triangles are selected. Thus \(U_C\) is open. The same argument applies to the complement, so \(U_C\) is also closed. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected.

The deck action preserves \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a leaf. Indeed, a leaf has endpoints \(b=(x_0,0)\) and \(t=(x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) preserves boundary components and translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it fixes the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) swaps boundary components; if it fixed the leaf, then
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible since \(\beta>0\).

Therefore the quotient
\[
\Lambda_M=\Lambda_\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\]
is a finite connected \(2\)-valent graph, hence a circle. The covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) has infinite degree, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Choose an orientation of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\). Since \(G_\beta\) acts freely on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), it is a fixed-point-free homeomorphism of \(\mathbb R\). Such a homeomorphism is orientation-preserving. After reversing the coordinate if necessary, we may assume \(G_\beta x>x\) for all \(x\). Thus the interval \([x,G_\beta x]\) is a fundamental interval for the action.

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). Because every leaf is lengthened by \(f\), its image is a nondegenerate segment with a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If \(\ell\) is a leaf, then \(G_\beta\ell\) has the same image segment but with the opposite orientation, because \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

We need the following elementary fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters modulo \(1\) whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented affine line by a unit direction vector \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend \((u(s),m(s))\) to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
so \(s\mapsto \phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), contradiction. Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, necessarily with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Apply the lemma to the oriented support lines along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). We obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) in \(M_\beta\) whose support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect. Let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

Because \(F\) is embedded and distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors, the image segments \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in one ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), hence has flat length at least \(1\). Since leaves are strictly lengthened,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let the endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\), and set
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Since \(\partial M_\beta\) has flat length \(2\beta\), we may write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the boundary circle by
\[
(u,0)\mapsto u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad (u,1)\mapsto u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus the coordinate difference between the two endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
s-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
The two boundary arcs between them have lengths \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), so
\[
s-\beta\equiv \beta\pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Hence
\[
s\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of this congruence class has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Therefore
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

Using boundary shortening on \(H\),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

Now use the arc \(D\), which contains \(q\). Since \(F(D)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\),
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Let \(y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1\). In the plane spanned by \(Q\) and the line \(AC\), place
\[
A=(-L/2,0),\qquad C=(L/2,0),\qquad Q=(u,y).
\]
Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=
\sqrt{(u+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(u-L/2)^2+y^2}.
\]
This expression is minimized at \(u=0\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(y>1\) and \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

## 2. Sufficiency: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad R=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
W^2-R^2=\frac{4\beta^2}{9}-\left(1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}\right)
=\frac{\beta^2}{3}-1>0,
\]
so \(W>R\). Choose
\[
R<\sigma<W,
\]
and define
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-R,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant triangulation

Define a \(9\)-periodic sequence of positive increments by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), hence every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\). Thus
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let \(Q_j\) be the quadrilateral with vertices
\[
X_j,\ X_{j+1},\ Y_{j+1},\ Y_j.
\]
For fixed height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
x_{j+9}(s)=x_j(s)+2\beta.
\]
Hence, for each \(s\), the intervals \([x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]\) tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Therefore the \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Since the widths are bounded below by a positive constant, the tiling is locally finite.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), producing clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]
The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So this is a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

In the quotient \(M_\beta\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(X_{j+9}=G_\beta^2(X_j)\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). The quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
\Theta_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane of \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small so that:

1. every distance between two points in the same cluster
\[
\{q_i(1,0),q_i(1-\eta,h),q_i(1+\eta,2h)\}
\]
is \(<\lambda/2\);

2. every distance between points in distinct clusters is in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4).
\]

This is possible by continuity, since as \(\eta,h\to0\), the \(i\)-th cluster collapses to \(p_i\), and the distances between distinct clusters tend to \(\sigma\).

Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extending affinely over each face \(\Theta_j\).

The nine image triangles are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\Theta_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(\Theta_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(\Theta_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(\Theta_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(\Theta_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

### 2.3. Embeddedness of the quotient map

The three horizontal triangles are
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1].
\]
The remaining six triangles form three side panels:
\[
R_{01}=[C_0,M_0,M_1]\cup[C_1,M_1,C_0],
\]
\[
R_{12}=[A_1,C_1,C_2]\cup[A_2,C_2,A_1],
\]
and
\[
R_{20}=[M_0,A_0,M_2]\cup[M_2,A_2,A_0].
\]

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to the triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\). For a horizontal plane of height \(z\), define
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}.
\]
Writing
\[
\alpha(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge \alpha(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\}.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) denote the side of \(\mathcal H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\). If \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=\alpha(r),\ \lambda_i,\lambda_j\ge\alpha(r)\}.
\]

Two elementary consequences will be used. First, adjacent sides with different scales are disjoint: if \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) met, then one would need both \(\alpha(r)\ge\alpha(s)\) and \(\alpha(s)\ge\alpha(r)\), hence \(r=s\). Second, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing,
\]
because \(\alpha(r)<\alpha(s)\).

The side-panel slices are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur, with scales \(>1\) and \(<1\), respectively. Their slices are adjacent sides with different scales, so they are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur. Their scales differ by
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0.
\]
Thus their slices are adjacent sides with different scales and are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the lower horizontal triangle meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\). At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\), while the slice of \(R_{12}\) has larger scale and is disjoint from that middle triangle. At \(z=2h\), the top horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet exactly along that diagonal. Since different heights are disjoint and the horizontal-slice analysis accounts for all possible intersections, the nine target triangles meet only along the edges and vertices prescribed by the quotient triangulation. Hence \(\Phi\) is injective. Therefore \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

### 2.4. Squeeze inequalities

We now verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in distinct clusters. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j,
\]
using \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Every nonboundary quotient edge is of the form
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation from the increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=R.
\]

The endpoints \(P_j,P_{j+5}\) lie in distinct clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le\sigma-R\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>R.
\]
Hence every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Thus the affine map on every quotient face is a squeeze map. Equivalently, the lifted affine map on every triangle \(D_j\) and \(E_j\) is a squeeze map.

### 2.5. The realizing map on \(\Sigma\)

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous. By the preceding subsection, \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends each triangle affinely to another triangle and both maps are affine on triangles, it follows that
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\Phi(\pi(p))=\Phi(\pi(q))
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).
